data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: July PMI Week, NZ Inflation And ECB's Rate Decision
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review: Cryptos on top and Decent US Data

It's been a roller‑coaster week-between volatile macro data, tariff threats, and fresh geopolitical turmoil, all jostling with record‑breaking crypto moves.

US inflation data eased a touch as CPI (core at 0.2% vs 0.3% exp) and PPI (unchanged, 0.2% consensus) both came in cooler than expected, while retail sales surprised to the upside, showing consumer demand still holding firm.

Equities briefly hit new highs - the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 climbed on solid earnings reports - but traders have been taking profits in today's session. Both indices still finish the week up comfortably, except for the Dow, which has really been mixed in rangebound action throughout the week.

Lower-than-expected (yet still high) inflation expectations did not do much to slow today's selling flows.

Global Equities have also been dawdling around in the first half of July as the sudden rise in the US Dollar coming from better than expected US data has rewired some financial flows.

Trade tensions resurfaced as Trump floated 15-20% tariffs on EU goods, a reminder that protectionist rhetoric remains ever‑present even as markets largely shrugged off the threat - keep this in check for the upcoming weeks.

Meanwhile, crypto stole the spotlight: Bitcoin blasted through to a new peak of $123,230, fuelled by ETF inflows and macro hedge demand, while Ethereum outpaced BTC on the week, riding a wave of DeFi optimism and relative strength. Altcoins have been shining on this newfound crypto trend.

Elsewhere, Australia's jobs report was disappointing, intensifying bets on RBA rate cuts, and the UK's employment data proved a mixed bag - wage growth held up, but unemployment ticked higher.

To cap it off, tensions flared along the Israel‑Syria border around Druze communities, adding a fresh geopolitical twist that quickly faded by markets that don't care too much about these headlines since the Israel-Iran tensions from the end of June.

Still, buckle up, as looking at charts and fundamentals, volatility isn't going anywhere.

The week ahead: Global PMIs and more inflation data

We will already be entering the fourth week of July, and markets have been waiting for the PMIs to get an idea if anything evolved for the business around the world as tariffs keep looming around.

Asia-Pacific Markets

Next week will see bouts of key data, particularly for New Zealand and Australia.

The week starts early for NZD traders with the New Zealand inflation (consensus + 0.6%) release on Sunday's overnight session.

The rest is considered slightly lower tier - PBOC Rate Decision, NZD Trade Balance data, Australian PMIs and RBA Minutes, and finally on Thursday, Tokyo region's Regional CPI for JPY Traders.

Asian stock markets (Singapore and Hong Kong) have been performing well on good Chinese data with the latest round of financial injection from the PBOC through Reverse Repo Ops (1 trillion yuan).

Economic Data from Europe, the UK and North America - ECB Rate Decision

Next week might be a bit heavier on occidental data releases with a slow start to the week (more action towards the middle of the week).

The week will start with mid-tier Business Outlook Surveys for Canada (typically interesting reports to get to know more about what businesses are expecting for inflation, etc.).

We will also get retail sales for Canada (exp -1.1%) on Wednesday and the UK (exp 1.2%) on Friday.

European data will be heavy with ECB's Bank Lending Survey (published once every 4 years), Eurozone, UK and German PMIs and finally, ECB's Rate Decision on Thursday 24 (pause largely expected) - (8:45).

Don't forget the main US Data release of the week - US Global Manufacturing and Services PMIs also on Thursday (9:45).

US Dollar performance versus other Fomajorajor counterparts

The US dollar kept rallying against other currencies throughout the week, particularly after the CPI report, which preceded a major rally with the DXY touching 98.90.

The AUD got sold off particularly strongly, and the JPY got dumped again, with USD/JPY attaining the extremes of its range.

Despite a rebound today, the price action looks a bit inconclusively bearish. Let's see what might happen next week (expect an in-depth analysis beginning next week).

Weekly Asset Performance

Ethereum's uprise had been warranted by its consistent rise in the past few weeks.

For the rest, despite volatile swings, the weekly performance is close to unchanged for most assets, except for oil, which lost some ground (rangebound action).

Stock indices have started to slow down a bit, so we'll need to track sentiment closely in the upcoming sessions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CDW Corporation: Strong Execution, But I'm Staying On The Sidelines For Now
data.description:
CDW Corporation (NASDAQ:CDW) is one of the biggest IT solutions providers in the US today, and it delivers everything from endpoint devices and networking equipment to cloud subscriptions, security tools, and managed services. Also, the company's massive vendor network and sales force are a big part of why it can serve everyone, from commercial and government customers to education and healthcare clients.

Let me summarize how FY2024 wrapped up. Roughly 45% of CDW’s net sales came from the Corporate segment, although its other segments performed fairly well. These days, too, the company's Services portfolio is becoming a bigger piece of the revenue pie, and that's positive.

But even with all that scale and execution firepower, I’m not buying the stock here. Q1 2025 results were very solid, really, but they came with a fair bit of front-loaded tailwinds. That includes tariff pull-forward demand. Now, we have another round of Trump-era tariffs looming, plus tighter federal IT budgets under the Department of Government Efficiency’s (DOGE) direction. All things considered, I can't say that the near-term setup screams “go all in” to me. CDW is a long-term compounder, yes, but at 22x earnings in the current macroeconomic environment, I’m holding off for now.

Q1 in Review: Strong Numbers with a Caveat

Let’s quickly look at Q1 2025’s numbers. Net sales came in at $5.2 billion, up 6.7% year over year, gross profit rose by 5.5%, and the margins stayed relatively stable at 21.6%. If we turn attention to the operating income, that was up ~10% to $361 million, and adjusted EPS climbed 10.6% to $2.15, both comfortably ahead of internal expectations. So far, so good. The company also generated $260 million in free cash flow, so we know that it can still convert profit into cash on a large scale.

However, I think the real story is in the breakdown. CDW’s commercial and small business segments grew nicely, helped in part by some front-loaded orders ahead of the updated China tariffs. Here's the commentary from management during that Q1 earnings call.

Moving on. Public and education spending also held up better than I expected. Having said that, I should also point out that even though revenue from sales to the federal government is a key contributor, sales here fell on a YoY basis. During the earnings call, management blamed tougher comparisons and slower procurement cycles, but it’s hard not to tie this to broader federal belt-tightening. I’ll discuss those in a later section.

TL;DR: Q1 was a good quarter, but it also might be the best one we get this fiscal year.

What I’m Watching In Q2 And Beyond

I’m holding CDW for now, but my attention is dialed into a few pressure points that can meaningfully shift sentiment over the next quarter or two.

Tariff Distortion Fallout: Will The Q1 Boost Become A Q2 Drag?

CDW’s Q1 topline held up decently, with growth in Commercial and Enterprise segments and solid demand for infrastructure and client devices. But as I mentioned above, management made it clear that part of that strength was due to customers pulling forward purchases to avoid higher input costs from the new tariffs. That means that Q1’s results were flattered by orders that may have otherwise landed in Q2 or Q3.

The latest wave of China-related import tariffs should kick in fully by Q3, and we might even see higher than expected rates because the administration is trying to pressure Russia’s allies (think China, Iran) to influence the former to agree to some sort of deal regarding the conflict between Russia and Ukraine. Those tariffs will likely include a broader set of electronics and networking components, and that gives me pause when trying to extrapolate this growth into Q2.

What’s the risk now? Customers bought ahead to lock in lower prices, so even though that demand hasn’t just disappeared, it does shift. That makes it harder to forecast near-term sales trends. This isn’t hypothetical either: we saw similar behavior back in 2018–2019 during the first wave of US-China trade skirmishes, where demand rose pretty sharply ahead of new duties, and then it all kind of flatlined for a couple of quarters.

I’m not suggesting anything close to a disaster, but the volume cadence might get lumpier, and we should expect some level of sequential deceleration in the device or networking categories. I don’t think it’s a function of structural weakness, but if you’re buying the stock, you need to decide whether a soft Q2 print is enough to doubt the business, especially because gross margins will be hit if CDW can’t pass on tariff costs quickly enough.

Budget Squeeze In Washington: Will Federal IT Spending Stay Resilient?

Generally speaking, public sector sales represent 35-40% of CDW’s total revenue in any given quarter, and it’s a reliable contributor with solid margins and stable contracts. The problem is that we’re now in a very different fiscal environment.

The Trump administration has made it a priority to slash discretionary spending and shrink civilian agency budgets. America is already six months into the president’s second term, and his administration has doubled down on budget tightening, especially across non-defense discretionary categories. DOGE (the name may have changed by now) has already recommended spending freezes and budget cuts in several federal departments. It’s not surprising, then, that we’ve already seen signs of that in Q1, where federal revenue came in soft despite Q1 usually being one of the stronger quarters due to fiscal calendar dynamics. CDW also says that its outlook for the rest of the year “...recognizes that our federal and education customers will need more time to adjust to recent government efficiency initiatives that will impact their IT planning, prioritization, and in some cases, their budgets.”

So, if the trend holds, federal IT spending will probably be subdued for the rest of this fiscal year, and that will have ripple effects across the company’s higher-margin offerings. To be clear, it doesn’t rely heavily on this segment, but the federal government is a lucrative customer, and if we see any sustained weakness here, it will weigh on both margins and investor sentiment.

In this climate, I’m not counting on a lot of growth from the public sector side. What I’m watching instead is how well CDW can protect its current federal business. Defense IT and cybersecurity tend to be more insulated, so those contracts might hold. But I wouldn’t be surprised to see delays in civilian tech procurement, slower software renewals, or a smaller scope with any new initiatives. We should also keep in mind that if CDW’s competitors start undercutting pricing to protect their wallet share, that will start putting some pressure on the company’s margins.

Cloud And Services Momentum: Hype Versus Durability

I think that one of the more encouraging developments in recent quarters is the company’s shift toward AI, services, and recurring revenue. Right now, it’s putting real weight behind Mission Cloud Services, a recent acquisition that is a premier AWS partner. Mission Cloud describes itself as “a leading born-in-the-cloud managed services and consulting provider, offering end-to-end cloud services, innovative AI solutions, and software for AWS customers.” Going off that, I’m not surprised that we also heard some strong commentary on cross-sell growth in software and security offerings.

All of those things are good, but what I’m watching now is whether those gains will be sticky or just cyclical. Mission Cloud sounds great on paper, but it competes in a very noisy space, and AWS doesn’t exactly have the greatest reputation when it comes to sidestepping resellers when it suits them. Meanwhile, a lot of the growth in SaaS and managed services is being driven by expansions from existing customers. What that means is that if enterprise IT budgets stay tight, that expansion motion could slow fast.

The question I’m asking: will we see a true flywheel form with higher retention, higher ACVs, and margin uplift from services mix, or does it plateau here? CDW is trying to build the modern IT marketplace, but markets need proof, not just branding.

It’s A Fair Valuation

At around $180 per share, CDW currently trades at a trailing P/E of ~21.8x and a forward P/E of 22x. Also, the stock has an EV/EBITDA multiple of ~14x, and that roughly tracks its 12‑month average. However, that multiple is a bit above the 12x mark that I’d associate with more value-oriented opportunities in this space.

At the same time, though, I think the P/S ratio around 1.1x is excellent because it reflects CDW’s status as a lean, recurring-revenue business. We’re not expecting much more from a high-growth software name, but you should know that it’s at a big discount to the sector median of 3.1x.

Here’s what it all means:

  • The earnings multiple is respectable, almost cheap, and it reflects the steady performance and quality execution we’ve seen so far.

  • The EV/EBITDA is fair value, relatively normal for a profitable, FCF-rich business.

Right now, we have a bit of a premium multiple relative to what the company’s short-term outlook is, and there is little room for surprises if Q2 shows tariff effects or softer federal/enterprise spending.

Risks to My Thesis

Prolonged spending pullbacks

If enterprise or government clients decide to wait out the macro or political uncertainty for longer than expected, then we may see CDW's rebound taking more than a couple of quarters. In my view, that would slightly weaken the case for sticking around.

Services growth stalls

A big part of the long-term upside I expect from this stock depends on the company scaling higher-margin services like Mission Cloud and professional offerings. So, if it happens that CDW is struggling to execute in those segments, we’re left with a lower-growth, margin-sensitive hardware distributor.

Tariff whiplash

If the tariff story stretches into Q3 and beyond, we will see an even bigger distortion in customer purchasing patterns. That will affect CDW’s ability to forecast and manage inventory, pricing, and channel dynamics effectively, and then it flows through to the margins and our outlook for pricing the stock properly.

My Recommendation

I’m staying on the sidelines for now. CDW’s not in bad shape, but between the tariff-induced lumpiness, federal budget uncertainty, and the pressure to deliver on services growth, it’s reasonable to say that there’s enough noise to justify caution. I want to see clean comps, stable demand trends, and more momentum on the margin front before moving off a Hold. So, I’ll revisit this when Q2’s earnings results are out.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 07/19/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Salesforce: Undervalued And Ready To Rebound On A Likely Q2 Beat
data.description:
My Past Coverage Of Salesforce

I wrote my very first bullish article on Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) back in July 2024, arguing that despite moderating growth rates of the business, the firm's margins looked stable, and CRM could receive an AI-driven boost in the next few years, so the dip in the stock price at the time was worth buying. Although CRM stock was up by about 45% since my rating initiation at some point, it eventually gave back all of its strength, and now it's basically flat on a YoY basis.

In my most recent article on CRM, dated back to March 2025, I argued that its dip was looking like a nice buying opportunity because of strong data cloud and AI-related revenues; I thought that it was going to recover thanks to a prospective margin expansion and overall growth, but CRM gave back ~11% of is market cap since that update of mine.

My Thesis Update

From what I can see today, CRM still looks too oversold, and I think its lagging momentum compared to the broader market makes it a "Buy" because the firm's addressable market should keep expanding over the next few years. Amid that, Salesforce's Agentforce platform is likely to drive more growth to the top line at reasonably high margins. If so, the long-term prospects look great for CRM, making the stock's valuation too cheap right now.

The firm is set to report for its fiscal Q2 2026 next month [8/27/2025], and while it may be too early to tell now, I think that it's very likely to beat the consensus again - that can become the long-awaited catalyst, which, I think, should help CRM finally move higher. I reiterate my "Buy" on CRM stock today.

Previewing Salesforce's Q2 FY2026 Earnings

Before jumping to the consensus estimates, or Q2, let's briefly take a look at the past Q1 data, as it's very critical for shaping out views on the upcoming earnings print. At consolidated sales of $9.83 billion (+8% YoY), CRM showed $2.58 in adjusted EPS (+5.73% YoY), thus beating on both the top and bottom lines. As CRM's customers adopt more clouds, the revenues from them become stickier, and the firm's attrition remains stable (just over 8% past quarter). So the cRPO (a well-known metric, "current remaining performance obligation", which indicates "the value of unearned revenue expected to be recognized within the next 12 months") went up by 12% YoY, potentially meaning that the future top-line expansion is going to be stronger than what we saw in Q1. The management raised the FY2026 guidance by $400 million (to $41.0-41.3 billion), which kind of supports the above assumption, in my understanding.

What I like to see beyond just revenues is the marginality, which is what CRM keeps in relatively strong condition. I mean, the firm's non-GAAP operating margin came in at 32.3%, and they guided for 34% for full FY2026. Effectively, I'd expect their operating leverage to raise EPS growth rates (vs. the sales growth rates) over the next few quarters. And it's exactly what the current consensus foresees: The next 12-quarter revenue and EPS CAGRs are estimated at 2.35% and 3.02%, respectively (calculated on a QoQ basis).

Now to the Q2 specifically: The management guided for $10.15 billion sales at the midpoint. The projected 8-9% YoY growth rate in Q2 is what they also see for the full-year FY2026, and I think there's an upside potential to that because the AI demand seems relatively strong nowadays.

Agentforce - CRM's digital labor platform - has already gone from 0 to 8,000 deals, half of which are paid deals, and all that over the past 6 months. Data Cloud business is the other half of the "AI-growth equation" as it now ingests over 22 trillion records (+175% YoY). When we combine Data Cloud and Agentforce, their ARR figure will be >$1 billion, growing more than 120% on a YoY basis, which is insanely strong, and while this combo isn't the biggest chunk of the revenue mix, I believe the underlying trend is there to stay. If so, the net-net effect on consolidated growth will likely be more and more visible over time.

Assuming that the Q1 trends in Agentforce and Data Cloud adoption among Salesforce's old customer base have been in active mode throughout the entire fiscal Q2 2026, the current consensus hardly captures the real impact because it's setting the bar even below the management's guidance (in terms of sales).

CRM has been expanding its sales team by 1,000–2,000 reps, with a focus on high-growth segments and geographies (APAC, Canada, France, UK), while also laying off some non-AI-focusing staff. In mid-May of this year, Salesforce introduced Flex - this is a new consumption-based pricing model, which should drive higher usage and stickiness over time, based on the commentary (the Q2 should already show its initial effects).

Overall, I like that the management raised the guidance for FY2026, and I like its projections of margin expansion. But what I think the Street is still not taking seriously in CRM's story is the company's potential growth reacceleration, which might come in the next few quarters thanks to their AI-related initiatives.

Valuation Update

As a quick sidenote, I should say that in general, the CRM stock has always been following the company's long-term revenue trend; of course, there were exceptions, but the general dynamics held pretty well. Right now, I think we're going through another cycle when the stock lags the fundamentals.

Of course, there's a reason for the current weakness - CRM isn't a growth stock anymore. But I don't think this fully justifies the stock's prolonged correction, as there are plenty of examples where large tech companies' growth momentum stalls and doesn't come close to double-digit growth rates, but their stock prices remain relatively stable. Recall Apple (AAPL) as a prime example. It's all about the premium on the valuation multiples investors are willing to pay, and how the management decides to approach shareholders' capital return policy. In CRM's case, we see that in Q1 alone, the firm spent ~$3 billion on buybacks and dividends and ~$24 billion since the program began. When the Q1 shareholders' return is annualized, we get a ~4.8% in shareholders' yield, which is pretty solid for the IT sector in general, in my opinion.

After the 23% YTD correction, the CRM stock is currently priced on par with the medians of the entire Information Technology sector, having a "D" grade based on Seeking Alpha's Quant system. That's not cheap based on this particular quant system, but it's already better than 3-6 months ago, and based on some FWD multiples we see that CRM already looks quite attractive.

Given that CRM's lack of business growth was the main trigger for the stock's correction, what I think we should mainly look at right now is CRM's forward PEG - the P/E ratio weighted by EPS growth rate, both on a forward-year basis. So, based on this metric, CRM is currently ~30% undervalued relative to the entire sector's median.

Looking at the next fiscal year, which is FY2027 in this case, we see that CRM's EPS should grow by almost 12%, based on today's consensus estimate. I'd put a 25x earnings multiple on that figure because I think the firm's strong FCFs and continued buybacks should eventually move it the multiple to the range of 24-26x. If I'm correct in my assumption, CRM's 12-month price target arrives at $315.75/share, which is ~21.44% above the current stock price.

Risks And Your Takeaway

There are some clear risks to my Buy thesis today, mainly flowing from the rising competition in the sector, as giants like Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), and others are investing heavily in AI, and they will do so in the foreseeable future, thus potentially pressuring the margin expansion opportunity for Salesforce. I also may be quite premature in calling the combination of Data Cloud and Agentforce as something that will help CRM reignite its top-line growth rates. If CRM fails to market these new offerings efficiently, my undervaluation findings will be pointless.

Despite the risks, I think that CRM has finally become a great GARP pick, even in today's volatile market environment. The market is pricing in sales and EPS figures for the upcoming Q2 below the figures I believe CRM can reach in reality. At the same time, CRM is trading at a 30% discount to the sector's median FWD PEG ratio, which is giving it more room for recovery if it beats earnings next month.

I decided to keep my "Buy" rating on CRM stock unchanged for now.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: Fruitful, But Growth Looks Limited
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome NM Market Insights as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (NASDAQ:SFM) has seen impressive gains in the last year with its stock doubling during the period. It traded at $165.65 as of July 18, 2025, up almost 30% year-to-date. Sprouts is an American grocery chain focusing on fresh, natural, and organic food. The company offers a curated selection of organic, plant-based, and gluten-free items in an open, farmer’s market-style layout, operating 443 stores in 24 states.

The kind of rally Sprouts has seen over the past year matters a great deal for investors, particularly at a time when the macroeconomic landscape is volatile, inflation remains sticky, and consumers are tightening their wallets. In fact, even the specialty grocer’s CEO, Jack Sinclair, sold a large number of shares on July 7th and 8th to lock in a gain.

While the company continues to post robust results and maintains confident guidance, a spate of insider selling by senior executives coupled with macro headwinds raises questions about how much upside is left. Moreover, its high valuation compared to peers is a good reason to pause on buying more. Given these factors, I recommend holding your position while closely monitoring upcoming financial results, and perhaps insider sales, over the next couple of quarters before reassessing.

A Look at Sprouts' Performance

Sprouts started 2025 on a strong note, with Q1 sales up 19% year-on-year to $2.2 billion. This was driven by an impressive 11.7% rise in comparable store sales. During the period, the grocer opened three new stores, which also performed well. Net income rose nearly 58% to $180 million from a year-ago, reflecting operational efficiency. For the full fiscal year, Sprouts projects sales growth between 12% and 14% compared with 13% last year. Comparable store sales are expected to come in between 5.5% to 7.5% for the whole year relative to 7.6% in the prior fiscal.

Operational cash flow rose to $299 million, up from $220 million a year earlier. The solid cash flow helped the company make $49 million in capital expenditure to support expansion. The company also returned $219 million to shareholders through the repurchase of 1.6 million shares.

With rising demand for organic products, the grocer is steadily increasing its footprint. In FY2024, it opened 33 stores and plans to open at least 35 new stores in the current fiscal.

Competitive Scene

As far as peers are concerned, Albertsons Companies Inc.'s (ACI) expanding organic offerings could intensify competition for Sprouts. Albertsons’ O Organics brand targets Gen Z and young millennials with greater scale and variety, and may attract price-conscious shoppers looking for more options. Sprouts also sees a market overlap with Trader Joe’s (privately held) with both grocers focusing on natural and organic food. However, the approach varies with Sprouts adopting an indoor farmer market style general store, while Trader Joe’s is known for its private label products. The company offers organic and specialty items, generally at competitive prices. Having said that, Sprouts’ tailored selection and strong loyalty among health-focused consumers may help it hold its ground.

What’s fueling Sprouts’ Strong Run

So, what’s behind Sprouts’ strong performance and deep customer loyalty? A Pew Research Center survey found that 90% of Americans are noticing higher costs for nutritious items and 69% are say increasing food prices are making it difficult to eat healthy. It's tough for lower-income individuals, but even middle- and upper-income Americans are feeling the impact. Despite this, Sprouts Farmers Market continues its strong performance.

There is a combination of factors that has been working well for the company. Clearly, Sprouts has understood the pulse of the market, and has structured an effective strategy, embracing health and wellness. It focuses on quality and freshness in the food it offers, which is targeted towards a particular segment of people – the health enthusiasts. Sprouts actively innovates and comes up with new items monthly, as evident from the 7,100 new items it launched in 2024. The company’s key strength comes from its attribute-driven products such as gluten-free, organic, and vegan groceries, organic, non-GMO, responsibly sourced seafood / meat, plant-based dairy, cage-free eggs, and so on. Attribute-driven products drive more than 70% of the sales.

While there’s no doubt that Sprouts well-thought-out offerings and innovative assortment play a big role in driving sales, an equally important factor is the kind of audience it targets. Sprouts appeals to a slightly younger, more educated, and higher-income demographic, many of whom are in dual-income households. This makes it less vulnerable to economic downturns. These customers place a high value on healthy eating, look for fresh, organic, and less processed food. Even when consumer confidence wavers, this group’s demand remains fairly inelastic.

In addition, awareness around healthy eating is steadily rising in the U.S., giving retailers like Sprouts Farmers Market a big boost. The U.S. health and wellness food market is projected to nearly grow from $250 billion in 2023 to $469 billion by 2030. This growing demand places Sprouts in a sweet spot – its focus on natural, quality foods align perfectly with evolving consumer preferences. As more Americans prioritize health and sustainability in their diets, Sprouts is well-positioned to expand both its existing customer base and future market footprint.

While these growth drivers paint a compelling picture, it's important to examine whether the current stock price fairly reflects this momentum or if investor enthusiasm has pushed valuations too far.

Strong Performance, But Valuation Signals Caution

Sprouts' growth has been secular and appealing to investors. If we expand our timeframe from one year to two years, the share price growth will look even more irresistible – increasing four times to $165.65.

The overall financial position of the company is hale and hearty. But in terms of valuation, Sprouts trades at significantly higher valuation multiples when compared to its peers.

Sprouts’ higher price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio and forward P/E reflect strong investor confidence in its future growth potential, given the company’s consistent financial performance and expanding market presence. The elevated price-to-sales (P/S) ratio also indicates investors paying a premium for each dollar of revenue in anticipation of continued momentum in top-line growth. A higher PEG ratio also suggests that the market views Sprouts as a retailer with long-term upside.

However, this optimism appears to have pushed the stock into overvalued territory. My DCF analysis shows that the stock appears priced ~20% above its intrinsic value based on fundamental cash flow projections. I applied a 6% free cash flow (FCF) growth rate, which is below the 4-year average of 8.69%. This is to conservatively account for fluctuations in free cash flow due to rising CAPEX, with FY2024 being the only year of FCF growth. I used a 2% terminal growth rate to reflect long-term economic maturity and moderate growth expectations.

While the DCF valuation indicates the stock may already be pricey, there's another layer worth examining. The behavior of Sprouts’ CEO and leaders, who know the company best. Recent insider transactions, particularly sustained selling by Sprouts’ CEO and senior leadership, add a note of caution that investors shouldn’t ignore.

Cashing In at the Top

CEO Jack Sinclair recently sold 8,090 shares of company stock across July 7 and 8, 2025, netting approximately $1.3 million. While these transactions were executed under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan, it took place at a time when company shares have seen a sharp ascent. Sinclair sold 4,045 shares each of these two days, with average sale prices of about $162.94 and $160.63. In addition, he exercised options to buy the same number of shares at just $16.47 a unit, totaling $133,242, thus making a substantial amount of real profit.

While insider sales can stem from various personal financial decisions, the timing aligns with Sprouts’ strong market momentum. This isn’t the first time the CEO has sold shares this year. In fact, he’s been consistently offloading stock almost every month, with the exception of April. He’s not alone either, several top executives have also been selling. While insider selling doesn’t always spell trouble, a pattern of frequent sales at the top may warrant some caution. It could be a subtle sign that the stock is already trading at a premium.

Risks

So, what are the risks to consider? Sprouts’ premium valuation means even small setbacks could weigh on the stock. Slowdown in same-store sales or rising costs could pressure margins. Its focus on a niche customer base – while a strength – also makes it sensitive to changes in consumer habits or a weaker economy. Lastly, sustained insider selling may dent investor confidence if not countered by strong operating results.

Holding Steady or Jumping In? Here’s My View

Several factors could move the needle for Sprouts. On the bullish side, continued strength in comparable store sales, steady store expansion, and consistent earnings beats, particularly amid a challenging macro environment could support further upside. On the bearish side, rising competition, earnings miss, margin pressure from inflation, or regular insider selling may shake investor confidence and weigh on the stock.

I'd suggest holding your position for now. If you already own shares, there’s no reason to hit the panic button as the company’s fundamentals look solid, and it's been performing well.

But if you’re thinking about jumping in, it might be better to sit tight for a bit. The recent insider selling and multiple transaction over the year is a little out of the ordinary and worth keeping an eye on. The strength of Sprout's resilient target consumer may also be tested this year of the economy slows enough to affect this demographic. With its strong run-up this year and some macro challenges ahead, today probably doesn't offer the best entry point.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Early BDC Earnings Look Fine
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up–highlighting individual news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of July.

Market Action

It was another strong week for BDCs with the average stock in our coverage rising nearly 2%. Month-to-date, the total average return is 4%. Only GAIN is down in July so far. HRZN outperformed, jumping 14% to an above-average valuation. This one remains a puzzle given it is one of the worst performers in the sector over the last 3 years.

The sector has nearly erased its post-Liberation Day meltdown. After lightening up on the positions acquired in the first half of April, we remain on the sidelines, keeping an eye on the coming earnings season.

The average valuation in our coverage is right on top of its historic level.

However, the median valuation remains below its historical average level, indicating opportunities remain in the sector.

Market Themes

Saratoga Investment Corp (SAR) is ringing the bell for the start of the BDC earning season. The company’s earning schedule is shifted earlier by a month which allows it to be a kind of canary in the coalmine.

Overall, the quarter was good. Adjusting for the extra dividend paid because of a shift to monthly dividends, the NAV was up 0.6%. After a tough 2024 with sizable realized losses, this past quarter there was a net realized / unrealized gain. Non-accruals remained low at 0.3% of fair value while NII fell slightly, adjusted for excise tax impact.

All in all, this is a good sign for the rest of the sector where earnings and portfolios should hold up well. The impact of tariffs is expected to be limited while flat short-term rates should support NII.

Market Commentary

BDCs like MAIN, TRIN, and HRZN have been announcing their new investment activity levels over Q2. TRIN said it originated $519m of new commitments in Q2 versus $186m in Q1. MAIN originated $196m in investments versus $130m in Q1. HRZN activity, however, fell in Q1 at $60m vs. $100m in Q1. The drop in HRZN activity is not surprising as the company’s leverage is elevated at 1.29x, in part due to previous losses.

Another driver of a recovery in new lending is seasonality. Q4 tends to see a lot of activity as managers race to finish deals before year-end which saps some business from Q1, making Q2 look strong by comparison.

Yet another reason could be a recovery in M&A which has been very depressed. We will know more from manager commentary during the earnings season if deal activity has actually risen from earlier depressed levels. Net net it’s somewhat better if overall commitments are elevated as that suggests that repayments are elevated as well, meaning portfolio turnover is high. The higher it is the more BDCs can make on fees for closing out and making new loans. It also suggests that there are more deals to choose from so BDCs can be pickier in their lending.

Elsewhere there was a comment that BDCs ought to match the duration of their assets to their liabilities. This doesn’t really make sense to us - BDCs are not insurance companies, so duration matching is not an obvious requirement there.

There are almost no BDCs that match the duration of their assets to their liabilities. The duration of BDC assets is close to zero, and the duration of liabilities is something like 2-3 given the combination of fixed-rate bonds and floating-rate credit facilities.

The key risk for BDCs would be if they fail to roll over their financing, particularly their credit facilities. You are kind of seeing that with PSEC which is issuing a lot of preferreds, so even if financing becomes hard to come by that could be an option as well for other BDCs.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: Financials Start Q2 '25 Earnings With A Bang; Expect Another Solid Quarter For S&P 500 Earnings
data.description:
This table has been posted a few times over the last 8 weeks, showing the progression of “expected” S&P 500 EPS and revenue growth rates for Q2 ’25.

Q2 ’25 earnings started this week, and all the large-cap financials' numbers slid: JPMorgan (JPM), Citigroup (C), Bank of America (BAC), Morgan Stanley (MS), Goldman Sachs (GS) and even American Express (AXP) this morning.

Note the darkened horizontal line on the left side of the above table: it shows that the expected Q2 ’25 EPS and revenue growth rates actually bottomed around the Memorial Day weekend, rather than the week of every quarter as is the typical pattern.

Note how with Q1 ’25 expected EPS growth rates, the Q1 ’25 EPS growth rate bottomed on April 4th ’25 (first column of data), versus how with Q2 ’25, the aforementioned bottoming of the growth rates was a full month earlier, around the end of May ’25.

Does this matter? This earnings growth rate pattern has been pretty steady for the last 15-20 years, so yes, the fact that the estimated growth rates bottomed a month earlier – in my opinion – shows a higher degree of confidence in the 2nd quarter ’25 EPS and revenue estimates.

While only 59 companies have reported Q2 ’25 results so far, (23 of the 59 companies reported are financial sector companies), the upside surprise is +7.2% and the biggest upside surprise – per sector – is financials at +10%. The revenue upside surprise for far in Q2 ’25 is +1.95%, also above average, but that figure will likely decline over the quarter.

The largest market cap weight within the financial sector in the S&P 500 is Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), which is up just 4.44% as of Thursday night, July 17th’s market close. The 2nd largest financial name in the S&P 500 by market cap weight is JPMorgan, and it was up 22.6% as of Thursday night’s market close.

Summary/conclusion

The early results for Q2 ’25 S&P 500 earnings look solid. Expect another decent quarter, although with the new August 1 deadline for tariffs, maybe the anxiety and angst now shift to the 3rd quarter of ’25. Or maybe tariffs shouldn’t have been a concern to begin with.

What’s shocking about the Q1 ’25 market drama is that the expected growth rate for the Q1 ’25 S&P 500 EPS growth rate bottomed at +7.8% in early April ’25, while the actual quarter-end growth rate peaked at +13.7%, or a 75% increase. Let’s see if Q2 ’25 estimates growth rates can beat Q1 ’25’s numbers.

None of this is advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IPAY: Superb Digital Payments Exposure
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome FinHeim Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Amplify Digital Payments ETF is an exchange traded fund managed by Amplify ETFs, with over $12 billion in assets across all of its ETF products and ranging different investment strategies for retail and institutional investors alike. IPAY itself has just shy of $300 million in AUM itself.

Before I dive in the details, why this ETF? And why would you invest in such an ETF when crypto and stablecoins are all the craze? There are fears that these kind of ETFs will be made redundant if stablecoins are issued by perhaps every company. Well, my thesis is that the perceived negative impact on these ETFs, especially IPAY, is overblown and that many of its holdings are poised to benefit from recent changes. This presents, in my opinion, an attractive buying opportunity.

We all know now more than ever, that cash as a transactional instrument is a dying breed, substituted by cards, online payments, mobile payments, and even crypto. Those substitutes are exactly what this ETF provides exposure to. Amplify Digital Payments ETF seeks to replicate the total return of the NASDAQ CTA Global Digital Payments Index, gaining exposure to global traditional companies in the payment space like Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA), and relatively new, disruptive companies in the industry such as crypto giant Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN), providing in my opinion the best balanced investment opportunity for retail investors.

Holdings

Going in depth, as of June 30th, 71% of the fund is comprised of large cap companies with a market cap over $10 billion. Mid-cap companies with a market cap in the range of $2-10 billion make up 25.56%, and small-cap companies represent the remaining 3.44%.

With regards to country allocation, the ETF is mostly allocated to US-based companies with over 81% of the holdings domiciled there, followed by the UK and the Netherlands coming in second and third place with 4.68% and 4.39%.

In terms of thematic allocation, as mentioned earlier, the fund brings into play a diversified set of industry players, with the bulk going to the Transaction & Payment Processing Services sector.

The fund is relatively concentrated within its top 10 holdings, with over 50% of the NAV attributed to those. the fund holdings numbering 37 as of July 15th. The expense ratio is 0.75%, somewhat higher than other thematic ETFs.

The Industry

Let’s first look at the market this ETF addresses. According to Fortune Business Insights, the global mobile payment market is valued at $3.84 trillion in 2024 and is forecasted to grow to $26.53 trillion by 2032, forecasting a staggering CAGR of 27%. Naturally having the highest population, the Asia Pacific region dominated the global market with a share of 45.31% in 2024. The increased coverage of high-speed mobile networks and the increase in smartphone usage are the main catalysts behind this kind of projected growth.

North America is the second largest market, with already developed and plentiful payment methods, attracting consumers through different strategies such as payback and reward systems. Europe as a market is traditional as in majority of the people use bank accounts and payment cards, and as such represents a big potential addressable market for innovative payment solutions. I have already seen such developments with partnerships between banks and payment providers boosting mobile based transactions. Further evidence of potential is the strategic decision made by PayPal Holdings Inc. (PYPL) to introduce near field communication (NFC) cards in the UK and Germany, among other European markets.

Moving on the dark cloud for the industry over the last few months: the idea that stablecoins can hamper the growth of some established companies in this industry. This worry led to a large dent in the ETF's performance this year relative to the broader market.

In the last couple of months, crypto and specifically stablecoins come to the forefront of investors’ attention through the GENIUS Act and, notably, the report last month that Walmart and Amazon, two of the biggest merchants are exploring how to issue stablecoins, potentially impacting high volumes of payments outside traditional financial system and affecting the payment ecosystem.

The news had an immediate impact and soured investor sentiment towards many payment giants, with Visa Inc. (V), Mastercard, PayPal and Block, Inc. (XYZ) falling sharply. This kind of exuberated reaction encompasses the jittery investor sentiment towards the industry in recent years. My opinion is that although stablecoins and crypto are the technology of the future, they pose a limited threat to the digital payment giants.

Healthy Perspective Is Warranted

Firstly, as previously mentioned, the traditional card system is just too practical to disrupt entirely. With consumers latching onto rewards such as cashbacks, they just don’t have the necessary incentives to change their behavior. I would like to also note that the major credit card companies and payment processors have already been building stablecoin capabilities, with PayPal pioneering the charge with the launch of PYUSD stablecoin in 2023. Most recently, Fiserv launched its own stablecoin, with the intention of helping its regional and community client banks preserve their market share.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, what I like about this ETF is that there are many companies that haven’t touched their 2021 highs even though revenue has risen quite a lot since (I acknowledge that the pandemic boom incorporated high expected growth, but in my opinion, the long term potential of this industry hasn’t lost its shine). This represents fresh gunpowder for years to come.

What’s notable is that all of the top 10 companies have double digit net profit margins and relatively lower ratios compared to their average in the early 2020’s. Traditional companies in the space such as Visa, Mastercard and American Express Company (AXP) provide the stability, while newer companies such as Coinbase, Block, Affirm Holdings Inc. (AFRM), Toast, Inc. (TOST) and others are expected to shape the future of payments.

Another aspect to consider is that the P/E of the fund is around 23x, while the S&P500 is at around 27x. I believe that in the near future, market sentiment will improve and will favor a convergence towards the market multiple, giving me short-term target of $70.

Risks

The risks to my thesis are that stablecoins capture a chunk of the market short-term, which would be visible in the near-term. As a result, transaction volumes may reduce for some of IPAY's largest holdings, compressing margins.

Also, a drop in interest rates would hurt margins for some of the larger, traditional companies. On the other hand, this could also have a positive effect regarding the discount rate and resulting valuation. Long term, the investment risk is slower adoption of digital payments, impacted by security concerns.

Conclusion

The IPAY Amplify Digital Payments ETF presents an attractive investment opportunity for the long term, offering exposure to the digital payments industry through traditional companies and growth companies. The current investor’s sentiment is overly negative and should improve as the clouds clear in the next few quarters regarding stablecoin adoption. Currently, this presents a good entry point.
